primary_diagnosis.name: Idiopathic small fibre neuropathy — acute flare with autonomic (orthostatic) involvement

pathophysiology_summary: Small fibre neuropathy involves degeneration of thinly-myelinated/unmyelinated fibres, producing burning stocking-glove pain, allodynia/hyperaesthesia and autonomic dysfunction (orthostatic intolerance, sweating changes), typically with preserved reflexes/power and a normal EMG. An acute flare causes unbearable burning pain and orthostatic dizziness. This mimics acute limb ischaemia, DVT and an ascending motor neuropathy (GBS). Reassuring features are warm, normally-perfused limbs with no single fixed white/cold/pulseless limb, preserved power with normal breathing/swallowing, and orthostatic dizziness that settles supine. However, telehealth cannot examine limbs/pulses, power/reflexes, or measure reliable postural vitals, and the fall risk is high. The correct action is urgent in-person neurological/vascular review plus fall-safety and supportive measures (bed cradle, cool room, positional advice), with low-dose neuropathic agents GP-initiated rather than started stat over chat, an outpatient work-up for secondary SFN causes, and 000 for a fixed pale/cold/pulseless limb, new true weakness/breathing difficulty, or true syncope.

differential.diagnosis: Small fibre neuropathy flare with dysautonomia
differential.evidence_basis: Burning stocking-glove pain with autonomic features on a known background.
Screen for a single fixed white/cold/pulseless limb.
Screen for ascending weakness and bulbar/respiratory involvement.
Bilateral burning without unilateral swelling is against DVT.

differential.diagnosis: Small fibre neuropathy flare with dysautonomia
differential.evidence_basis: Warm perfused limbs, preserved power, no ascending weakness — an SFN flare with autonomic involvement.
Low probability but not excludable without limb examination/pulses.
Retained pending exam if any weakness/breathing change appears.

differential.diagnosis: Small fibre neuropathy flare with dysautonomia
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: SFN flare with autonomic involvement needing urgent in-person neuro/vascular review, fall-safety, supportive measures and a secondary-cause work-up.

red_flags.description: A single limb becoming fixed white, cold, numb or pulseless (acute limb ischaemia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute arterial occlusion threatens the limb and needs emergency care.

red_flags.description: New ascending motor weakness, or difficulty breathing/swallowing (GBS)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ascending paralysis with respiratory involvement is a neurological emergency.

red_flags.description: True syncope / high fall risk from dysautonomia while isolated
red_flags.clinical_significance: Near-syncope with pain-immobility and hazards risks injurious falls.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening for a single ischaemic limb or an evolving ascending motor neuropathy.
Starting amitriptyline stat over telehealth in a dysautonomic, near-syncopal patient.
Not addressing near-syncope/fall risk and hazards while isolated.
Not arranging investigation for treatable causes of small fibre neuropathy.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Warm perfused limbs, preserved power; postural HR rise/BP drop — SFN flare with dysautonomia
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports urgent in-person neuro/vascular review with supportive management and fall-safety.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Neurological examination (power, reflexes, sensation) and vascular examination/pulses
Nerve/skin-biopsy and blood work-up for secondary causes
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen limb-ischaemia/GBS and orthostatic features; advise supportive measures (bed cradle, cool room, positional advice) and fall-safety; arrange urgent in-person neuro/vascular review and a secondary-cause work-up; escalate to 000 for a fixed pale/cold/pulseless limb, new true weakness/breathing difficulty, or true syncope.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Attributing everything to SFN and starting a tricyclic remotely could miss limb ischaemia/GBS or precipitate hypotension/falls.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Neuropathic pain is managed with GP-initiated, titrated agents (e.g. amitriptyline, gabapentinoids, duloxetine); investigate for secondary causes of neuropathy.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Vascular guidance on acute limb ischaemia
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: A pale, cold, pulseless limb is a surgical emergency requiring urgent revascularisation.
